name: Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop
name_original: Parmu, Cueva Huerta, Embalse de Valdemurrio, Puerto Alto de Ventana – circular en Las Ubiñas-La Mesa
description: Tackle the difficult Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop, a 38.3-mile mountain biking route with 5955 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Mountain biking around Teverga offers diverse terrain within the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park, a designated Biosphere Reserve. The region is characterized by the rugged Asturian Mountains, dense forests of beech and oak, and dramatic geological formations like vertical limestone walls and gorges. This natural setting provides a variety of mountain bike trails suitable for different skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Teverga The most popular mountain bike trail is Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop, a 38.3 miles (61.6 km) trail that takes 5 hours 27 minutes to complete. This challenging route incorporates the Senda del Oso, an old mining railway line. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Xiblu Waterfall and Huerta Cave Loop, a difficult 17.8 miles (28.7 km) path. This trail leads through the Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, passing the Xiblu Waterfall. Local mountain bikers also love the Peñas Juntas Gorge – Stone Bridge of Proaza loop from La Plaza, a 31.5 miles (50.8 km) trail leading through the Peñas Juntas Gorge, often completed in about 4 hours 15 minutes. Mountain biking around Teverga is defined by the Asturian Mountains, dense forests, and dramatic limestone walls and gorges. The network offers options for experienced riders seeking challenging ascents and technical descents. The routes in Teverga are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 30 reviews. More than 500 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Teverga's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are there in Teverga?
A: Teverga offers a selection of 12 mountain bike trails, with options ranging from moderate to difficult. The region is almost entirely natural, providing a rugged and scenic environment for mountain biking within the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of mountain bike trails in Teverga?
A: The mountain bike trails in Teverga are predominantly challenging, with 11 out of 12 routes rated as difficult. There is one moderate route available, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10503839" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puertos de Marabio – Puertos de Marabio loop from La Plaza&lt;/a&gt;. Riders should expect significant elevation gains and technical sections.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails in Teverga?
A: While most routes in Teverga are rated difficult, the region is known for the Senda del Oso (Bear Trail), which is generally considered suitable for all ages and skill levels. This trail follows an old mining railway line, offering a less strenuous ride through scenic landscapes. However, specific komoot routes listed here are more challenging, so families might need to seek out dedicated sections of the Senda del Oso or other local, easier paths not detailed in this guide.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on Teverga's mountain bike trails?
A: Teverga's mountain bike trails traverse diverse terrain within the Asturian Mountains. You'll encounter dense forests of beech, oak, chestnut, and birch, rugged mountain paths, and dramatic geological formations including vertical limestone walls and gorges. The routes often feature significant elevation changes, characteristic of the Cordillera Cantábrica.

Q: Are there any notable natural landmarks or viewpoints along the mountain bike routes?
A: Yes, Teverga's trails offer access to several natural wonders. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027481690/xiblu-waterfall-and-huerta-cave-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Xiblu Waterfall and Huerta Cave Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the impressive Xiblu Waterfall. You can also experience the spectacular views of the Peñas Juntas Gorge on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31432653" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas Juntas Gorge – Spectacular View loop from La Plaza&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also home to the Montegrande Hayedo (beech forest) and the dramatic Peña Sobia limestone walls.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about Teverga?
A: The mountain bike trails in Teverga are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 30 reviews. Riders often praise the stunning natural beauty, the challenging and rewarding ascents, and the immersive experience within the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park, a designated Biosphere Reserve.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Teverga?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes in Teverga are designed as loops. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027486695/ventana-pass-and-senda-del-oso-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 61.6 km, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31437800" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas Juntas Gorge – Stone Bridge of Proaza loop from La Plaza&lt;/a&gt;, a 50.8 km route.

Q: What is the best time of year to go mountain biking in Teverga?
A: Teverga offers a vibrant, ever-changing landscape throughout the seasons due to its diverse deciduous forests. While specific recommendations depend on personal preference, spring and autumn often provide pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be warm, and winters may bring snow to higher elevations, especially within the Asturian Mountains.

Q: Are there any caves or mountain passes to explore near the trails?
A: Yes, the area around Teverga features several caves and mountain passes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027481690/xiblu-waterfall-and-huerta-cave-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Xiblu Waterfall and Huerta Cave Loop&lt;/a&gt; passes by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3592942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huerta Cave&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable caves include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6350742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuevallagar Cave&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7283768" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abrigo del Paso&lt;/a&gt;. For mountain passes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt; is a significant feature, and others like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2873221" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Lorenzo Pass&lt;/a&gt; are also in the vicinity.

Q: Is Teverga a good destination for wildlife spotting while mountain biking?
A: Teverga is an excellent destination for wildlife enthusiasts. As part of the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park and a Biosphere Reserve, it's a significant breeding ground for western bears. You may also spot deer, wild boar, and hares, particularly in the extensive forest and pastureland that makes up over 90% of the municipality.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the mountain bike trails in Teverga?
A: While the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park encourages responsible outdoor recreation, specific regulations regarding dogs on mountain bike trails can vary. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife, especially given the presence of western bears and other animals. Always check local signage or park authority guidelines for the most current information.

Q: Are there any cultural attractions near the mountain bike trails in Teverga?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, Teverga offers cultural attractions. The Park of Prehistory, also known as the "Cave of Caves," features recreations of Paleolithic caves and significant European cave paintings. The Collegiate Church of San Pedro de Teverga also houses historical artifacts, including the mummies of the Marquees of Valdecarzana and his son.
tips: A really nice stretch of road. It is not usually very busy with motor vehicles, even so, be alert at every curve: with such beauty it is easy to get lost and change lanes.
After the climb from Cueva Huerta follow the Entrigo sign to the right to take the Roman DH way.
Mandatory stop. It has a walkway that crosses the Senda del Oso. It is a very beautiful place with wonderful views. There is a bar if you fancy something (closes at 7pm).

The ''Pista de Ricabo'' is a very nice gravel road, with hardly any traffic, that runs through a wooded area to Puerto de Ventana. There is a 800m ascent (or descent) that will take you through the trees and, most likely, the mist, revealing beautiful panoramic views at the top. The differences between Asturias and León can be seen: Asturias, always humid and leafy, and León, more arid and drier.

Puerto de Ventana is a mountain pass located in the Cantabrian Mountains, between the Principality of Asturias and the province of León. It lies at an altitude of 1,587 meters and connects the municipality of San Emiliano in León with the Asturian municipality of Teverga.

From the pass, you can enjoy a breathtaking view of the San Emiliano Valley, known as Babia de Yuso.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop mountain bike trail?
faqs.answer: This mountain bike route is rated as difficult. While it incorporates sections of the generally easy Senda del Oso greenway, it also includes the challenging ascent to Ventana Pass, which features significant elevation gain and can involve dirt tracks, making it suitable for experienced mountain bikers.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied. You'll encounter sections of the Senda del Oso, which is a rail-trail with easy gradients, often paved or well-maintained. However, the route also includes more challenging ascents and descents, particularly around Ventana Pass, where you can expect steeper gradients and potentially unpaved or dirt track sections.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes several interesting points. You'll go by the Huerta Cave, the scenic Valdemurio Reservoir, and the challenging Ventana Pass. The Senda del Oso section is also known for its numerous tunnels and bridges, as well as the bear enclosure in Proaza where you might spot Cantabrian brown bears.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this loop?
faqs.answer: The best time to ride this loop is generally during spring, summer, and early autumn. During these seasons, the weather is more stable, and the trails are less likely to be affected by snow or heavy rain, especially at higher elevations like Ventana Pass. Winter riding can be challenging due to snow and colder temperatures.

faqs.question: Is the Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Senda del Oso section is generally popular with walkers and cyclists, including those with dogs, the overall difficulty and length of this specific loop, especially the challenging Ventana Pass section, might not be suitable for all dogs. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-conditioned for long, difficult rides and always keep it on a leash, especially near wildlife areas like the bear enclosure.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or is there an entrance fee for this trail or the surrounding natural park?
faqs.answer: No specific permits are generally required for mountain biking this route. The trail passes through the Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, which does not typically charge an entrance fee for general access. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and wildlife.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop?
faqs.answer: Given that the route incorporates parts of the Senda del Oso, you can find parking in towns along that greenway, such as Proaza, Entrago, or Tuñón. For the full loop, consider starting points that offer convenient access to both the Senda del Oso and the ascent towards Ventana Pass. Some towns may offer dedicated parking areas for trail users.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route utilizes significant portions of the Vía Verde Senda del Oso and also includes sections of the [CIMA AS01] Ventana. You will also find it briefly intersects with the Asturias Interior (Etapa 15) and the Camín Real del Puertu Ventana o Ruta de las Reliquias.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or bike rentals near the trail?
faqs.answer: Along the Senda del Oso section, particularly in towns like Proaza or Entrago, you'll find various amenities including cafes, restaurants, and bike rental services. These services cater to the many visitors enjoying the greenway. However, facilities become scarce as you ascend towards Ventana Pass, so plan accordingly.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on this mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: The most famous wildlife attraction is the bear enclosure in Proaza along the Senda del Oso, where you can observe Cantabrian brown bears. Beyond that, as the route traverses the Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, you might spot various bird species, deer, and other local fauna, especially in the quieter, more remote sections.

faqs.question: Is this loop suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: While the Senda del Oso itself is very family-friendly and suitable for children, the full 'Ventana Pass and Senda del Oso Loop' is rated as difficult due to its significant elevation gain and challenging sections. This specific loop is generally not recommended for families with young children or beginner riders. Consider riding only the easier, flatter sections of the Senda del Oso if cycling with family.

waypoints.name: 800 m of Roman Way and DH (descent)
